title: Your body, a dwelling place
passage: Or don’t you know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you, whom you have from God? You are not your own, for you were bought with a price. Therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit, which are God’s.
reflection: Training and nutrition become acts of stewardship when you remember you are not your own—you were bought with a price. Let today’s choices honor that truth without turning food or the mirror into an idol.
studyPrompt: Where is it hardest for you to “glorify God” with your body this week—rest, food, or pushing too hard?

title: Whatever you do
passage: And whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men, knowing that from the Lord you will receive the reward of the inheritance; for you serve the Lord Christ.
reflection: Sets, reps, meal prep, and sleep are not neutral—they can be offered to the Lord. Showing up with integrity in the gym and kitchen is a form of worship when your motive is love for Christ, not vanity.
studyPrompt: What part of your routine feels most “for people” instead of “for the Lord”? One small shift you could make today?

title: Discipline that yields peace
passage: All discipline seems for the present to be not joyful, but grievous; yet afterward it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it.
reflection: Soreness, hunger on a cut, and boring consistency are forms of discipline. Scripture promises a harvest—peace and righteousness—not just a smaller waist. Stay the course.
studyPrompt: What discipline is “grievous” right now? Ask God for endurance, not just results.

title: Strength for the weary
passage: He gives power to the weak. He increases the strength of him who has no might. Even youths faint and get weary, and the young men utterly fall; but those who wait for Yahweh will renew their strength.
reflection: Burnout is real. Your strength is not unlimited—but God’s is. Waiting on him is active trust: sleep, Sabbath, and humility about your limits are spiritual practices too.
studyPrompt: Do you need to “wait” more—less grinding, more trust—this week?

title: Run with perseverance
passage: Let us run with perseverance the race that is set before us, looking to Jesus, the author and perfecter of faith.
reflection: Faith fixes your eyes on Jesus, not the leaderboard. Perseverance in fitness mirrors perseverance in faith: both require showing up when motivation fades.
studyPrompt: What distraction pulls your eyes off Jesus when you train or eat?

title: Self-control
passage: The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faith, gentleness, and self-control.
reflection: Self-control is Spirit-produced fruit, not white-knuckled willpower alone. Ask for help with cravings, comparison, and skipping rest.
studyPrompt: Where do you need the Spirit’s self-control today—plate, phone, or pace?

title: Do not worry
passage: Don’t be anxious for your life, what you will eat, or what you will drink; nor yet for your body, what you will wear. Isn’t life more than food, and the body more than clothing? See the birds of the sky.
reflection: Anxiety about food, scale weight, or aesthetics can crowd out trust. Jesus invites a lighter grip: seek his kingdom first; your Father knows what you need.
studyPrompt: What fitness worry can you hand to God in prayer before bed?

title: Renewed day by day
passage: Therefore we don’t faint, but though our outward man is decaying, yet our inward man is renewed day by day.
reflection: Bodies age and ache; that is not failure. Inner renewal is daily mercy. Train with gratitude for what your body can still do today.
studyPrompt: Thank God for one thing your body did for you yesterday.

title: Commit your work
passage: Commit your deeds to Yahweh, and your plans shall succeed.
reflection: Offer your programming, meals, and recovery plans to God. Success is faithfulness with what he entrusts you—not perfect metrics.
studyPrompt: What plan will you explicitly commit to God before you execute it today?

title: Be strong and courageous
passage: Haven’t I commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Don’t be afraid, neither be dismayed, for Yahweh your God is with you wherever you go.
reflection: Heavy lifts and hard conversations both call for courage. God’s presence is the reason you don’t have to fear failure or judgment.
studyPrompt: What step feels scary today—nutrition, a PR attempt, or rest? Name it before God.

title: Thanks in all circumstances
passage: Rejoice always. Pray without ceasing. In everything give thanks, for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus toward you.
reflection: Thanksgiving reframes plateaus: gratitude for health, access to food, and the ability to move is a workout for the soul.
studyPrompt: List three fitness-related gifts you rarely thank God for.

title: I can do all things
passage: I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me.
reflection: This is contentment in Christ’s strength—not a promise of unlimited PRs. His strength sustains you in deficit, injury rehab, and obedience.
studyPrompt: Where do you need Christ’s strength more than your own today?

title: Wisdom in number of days
passage: So teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom.
reflection: Your days are finite; wisdom chooses what matters. Training fits inside a life lived for God, not the other way around.
studyPrompt: Is fitness serving your calling—or crowding it? One boundary to protect what matters.

title: Love the Lord with all your strength
passage: You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind; and your neighbor as yourself.
reflection: Physical strength can be offered in love—serving others, showing up for family, having energy for good works. Don’t waste it only on self.
studyPrompt: How could you use your strength to love a neighbor this week?

title: Present your bodies
passage: I urge you, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable to God, which is your spiritual service.
reflection: Holistic worship includes sleep, food, and movement. A living sacrifice is daily—not a one-time diet start date.
studyPrompt: What does “present your body” look like in today’s schedule?

title: The Lord is my shepherd
passage: Yahweh is my shepherd; I shall lack nothing. He makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters. He restores my soul.
reflection: Green pastures and still waters are God’s idea of care. Rest and recovery are shepherding, not laziness.
studyPrompt: When did you last let God “make you lie down”? Plan one real rest block.

title: Train yourself for godliness
passage: Bodily exercise has some value, but godliness has value in all things, having promise of the life which is now and of that which is to come.
reflection: Paul affirms exercise has value—just not ultimate value. Keep fitness in its lane: a good servant, a poor god.
studyPrompt: Where might fitness be creeping toward “ultimate” in your heart?

title: Cast your anxiety
passage: Casting all your worries on him, because he cares for you.
reflection: Scale weight, injuries, and comparison are easy to carry alone. God cares—transfer the weight in prayer before you scroll or step on the scale.
studyPrompt: Name one worry to cast on God right now in a sentence of prayer.
